espaco-cultural-da-marinha.name: Navy Cultural Space (Espaço Cultural da Marinha)
espaco-cultural-da-marinha.short_description: Naval museum of the Brazilian Navy on the shore of Guanabara Bay, with museum ships and historic vessels.
espaco-cultural-da-marinha.address: Avenida Alfredo Agache, s/n — Downtown (waterfront, near Praça XV)
espaco-cultural-da-marinha.admission: Check prices on the Navy's official website

opening_hours.obs: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: The **Navy Cultural Space (Espaço Cultural da Marinha)** is a naval museum run by the Brazilian Navy, on the downtown waterfront near Praça XV. It gathers the Navy's historic collection and vessels open to visitors, such as museum ships and the royal galliot used by the imperial family. It tells the story of navigation and Brazil's naval defense, with rooms on underwater archaeology and the great voyages.
curiosities: Houses museum ships, such as the submarine Riachuelo, and historic Navy vessels.
Sits on the shore of Guanabara Bay, part of the Praça XV circuit.
practical_tips: Great to combine with Praça XV, the Paço Imperial and the National Historical Museum.
Confirm hours and prices on the official website — they may change.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV or Antônio Carlos–MAM station.
On the downtown waterfront, near Praça XV.

submarino-museu-riachuelo.name: Riachuelo Submarine Museum
submarino-museu-riachuelo.short_description: An Oberon-class submarine built in England in the 1970s, now open to visitors at the Navy Cultural Space.
submarino-museu-riachuelo.address: Av. Alfredo Agache, s/n — Downtown (Navy Cultural Space)
submarino-museu-riachuelo.admission: Check prices on the Navy's official website

opening_hours.obs: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — hours may change; confirm on the Navy's official website.

content.history: The **Riachuelo Submarine Museum (S-22)** was built by the **Vickers** shipyard in Barrow-in-Furness, England, and belonged to the **Oberon class** (in Brazil, the Humaitá class). Its keel was laid in **1973**, it was launched on **6 September 1975** and commissioned into the **Brazilian Navy in 1977**. It served for about **20 years**, sailing more than **181,000 nautical miles**, until it was decommissioned on **12 November 1997**. Since then it has been open to visitors as a submarine museum at the **Navy Cultural Space**, in downtown Rio.
curiosities: It is a real Oberon-class submarine, built in England in the 1970s.
It sailed more than 181,000 nautical miles over about 20 years of service.
Visitors walk through the interior, seeing the engine room and the crew compartments.
practical_tips: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — confirm hours and prices on the official website, as they may change.
The interior is narrow; it may not be suitable for people with claustrophobia or reduced mobility.
Combine it with the Bauru Museum Ship and Praça XV, right next door.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station.
On the downtown waterfront (Av. Alfredo Agache), inside the Navy Cultural Space, near Praça XV.

navio-museu-bauru.name: Bauru Museum Ship
navio-museu-bauru.short_description: A WWII destroyer escort (former USS McAnn), now a museum ship at the Navy Cultural Space.
navio-museu-bauru.address: Av. Alfredo Agache, s/n — Downtown (Navy Cultural Space)
navio-museu-bauru.admission: Check prices on the Navy's official website

opening_hours.obs: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — hours may change; confirm on the Navy's official website.

content.history: The **Bauru Museum Ship** is a **Cannon-class destroyer escort** (in Brazil, the Bertioga class), built in the United States during **World War II**. It was launched on **15 September 1943** and commissioned into the US Navy as **USS McAnn (DE-179)**. On **15 August 1944** it was transferred to the **Brazilian Navy** and named **Bauru**, after the city in São Paulo state. It took part in **convoy escort in the South Atlantic** during the war. After about **37 years** of service, it became a museum ship, inaugurated in **1982** (at Marina da Glória) and moved to the **Navy Cultural Space** when it opened, in **1996**.
curiosities: It was originally USS McAnn (DE-179), of the United States Navy.
It escorted convoys in the South Atlantic during World War II.
It was one of eight destroyer escorts transferred by the USA to Brazil in the war effort.
It is preserved with the appearance it had in World War II.
practical_tips: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — confirm hours and prices on the official website.
You can walk along the deck and through the ship's compartments.
Combine it with the Riachuelo submarine and Praça XV.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station.
On the downtown waterfront (Av. Alfredo Agache), inside the Navy Cultural Space, near Praça XV.

nau-dos-descobrimentos.name: Nau dos Descobrimentos (Ship of Discoveries)
nau-dos-descobrimentos.short_description: A replica of a ship from the Age of Discovery, on display on the waterfront of the Navy Cultural Space.
nau-dos-descobrimentos.address: Av. Alfredo Agache, s/n — Downtown (Navy Cultural Space)
nau-dos-descobrimentos.admission: Check prices on the Navy's official website

opening_hours.obs: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — hours may change; confirm on the official website.

content.history: The **Nau dos Descobrimentos (Ship of Discoveries)** is a **replica of a Portuguese nau** from the **Age of Discovery** (15th–16th centuries), on display on the waterfront of the **Navy Cultural Space** in downtown Rio. The vessel helps tell the story of **navigation and the great sea voyages** that connected Europe, Africa and Brazil, and is part of the historic-vessel circuit kept by the Navy on the shore of Guanabara Bay.
curiosities: It is a replica evoking the naus of the 15th- and 16th-century Age of Discovery.
It sits on the waterfront, part of the Navy Cultural Space's set of vessels.
It helps tell the story of the sea voyages between Europe, Africa and Brazil.
practical_tips: Visited through the Navy Cultural Space — confirm hours and conditions on the official website.
Great for photos on the shore of Guanabara Bay.
Combine it with the Riachuelo submarine, the Bauru Museum Ship and Praça XV.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station.
On the downtown waterfront (Av. Alfredo Agache), at the Navy Cultural Space, near Praça XV.

tribunal-maritimo.name: Maritime Court (Tribunal Marítimo)
tribunal-maritimo.short_description: A court specialized in maritime law, created in 1934, on the downtown waterfront of Rio.
tribunal-maritimo.address: Av. Alfredo Agache, s/n — Downtown (near Praça XV)

opening_hours.obs: Public body (not a tourist visit) — open on business days.

content.history: The **Maritime Court (Tribunal Marítimo)** is a body **auxiliary to the Judiciary**, specialized in **maritime law**. It was created by **Decree No. 24,585 of 5 July 1934**, with its seat in **Rio de Janeiro** — then the federal capital. Administratively linked to the **Brazilian Navy**, it judges **accidents and facts of navigation** (such as shipwrecks, collisions and groundings), determining their causes, circumstances and those responsible. It stands on the downtown waterfront, on Avenida Alfredo Agache, near Praça XV and the Navy Cultural Space.
curiosities: It was created in 1934 and has now passed 90 years of operation.
It judges accidents and facts of navigation, such as shipwrecks and collisions.
It is linked to the Brazilian Navy but works as a body auxiliary to the Judiciary.
practical_tips: It is a public body, not a tourist attraction.
It sits on the same waterfront as the Navy Cultural Space and Praça XV.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station.
On Avenida Alfredo Agache, on the downtown waterfront, near Praça XV.

maternidade-oswaldo-nazareth.name: Oswaldo Nazareth Maternity Hospital
maternidade-oswaldo-nazareth.short_description: A former public maternity hospital at Praça XV, a downtown landmark for decades; closed since 2013.

content.history: The **Oswaldo Nazareth Maternity Hospital**, known as the **Praça XV Maternity**, was for decades a **public maternity hospital** of reference in downtown Rio de Janeiro, at **Praça XV de Novembro, 4**. It was recognized for the quality of its care for mothers and babies and for its **breastfeeding-support programs**. The unit was **closed in 2013**, and its services were transferred to other municipal maternity hospitals, such as the Maria Amélia Buarque de Hollanda Maternity.
curiosities: It was known as the Praça XV Maternity.
It was a reference for breastfeeding-support programs in Rio.
It operated for decades downtown and was deactivated in 2013.
practical_tips: The maternity has been closed since 2013 — there is no service at the site.
In an emergency, look for a health unit that is in operation.
It sits at Praça XV, near the Navy Cultural Space and the Paço Imperial.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station.
At Praça XV de Novembro, downtown, near the Paço Imperial and the Navy Cultural Space.

cais-do-valongo.name: Valongo Wharf
cais-do-valongo.short_description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site. The largest port of entry for enslaved Africans in the Americas, rediscovered in 2011.

content.history:
The Valongo Wharf is one of the most significant sites in the history of the African diaspora in the world. Built in 1811 to receive enslaved Africans arriving in Brazil, it replaced the former landing point at Prainha (today's Praça XV). Between 500,000 and 1 million people passed through here, mainly from West and Central Africa — the largest number of enslaved Africans to arrive in the Americas through a single point.

The name 'Valongo' comes from the expression 'Vale Longo' (Long Valley), referring to the path that led to the nearby slave market.

In 1843 the wharf was renovated and renamed 'Cais da Imperatriz' (Empress's Wharf) to receive Princess Teresa Cristina of Bourbon-Two Sicilies, who came from Italy to marry Emperor Dom Pedro II. The original pavement was covered with marble slabs. In 1911, during Mayor Pereira Passos's great urban reform, the whole area was filled in and the wharf buried, vanishing from memory for a century.

In 2011, during the Porto Maravilha works to revitalize the port area, workers uncovered the wharf's original stones about 1 meter underground. The discovery was immediate and sensational — archaeologists found not only the stones but personal objects of the Africans: amulets, cowrie shells, pipes, fragments of African pottery and coins.

IPHAN (the National Historic and Artistic Heritage Institute) granted heritage protection to the site in 2013. On 9 July 2017, during the 41st session of the World Heritage Committee held in Kraków, Poland, the Valongo Wharf was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List under Criterion VI — direct association with events and traditions of universal significance related to human rights violations.

On 11 September 2025, President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva signed Law No. 15,203/2025, formally recognizing the archaeological site as 'Afro-Brazilian historical and cultural heritage essential to the formation of the national identity'.
curiosities: UNESCO describes Valongo as 'the most important material vestige of the arrival of enslaved Africans in the Americas'.
During the 2011 excavations, religious objects of African origin (cowrie shells and amulets) were found, indicating that the enslaved performed spiritual rituals even at the moment of landing.
In 1843 the wharf was literally hidden under marble to erase the memory of slavery before the empress Teresa Cristina arrived.
The name 'Valongo' appears in 19th-century European travelers' accounts that described the conditions of the place with horror.
Archaeological research revealed that Africans from at least 9 different ethnic groups passed through Valongo, identified by the styles of the objects found.
Law 15,203/2025 was a historic milestone: for the first time, a Brazilian federal law explicitly recognized Valongo as Afro-Brazilian cultural heritage.
practical_tips: The visit is free and open-air — the archaeological site is exposed on the sidewalk.
Combine it with a visit to the Pretos Novos Research and Memory Institute, 200m from Valongo.
On Sundays, volunteer guides offer free guided tours.
The Orla Conde tram (VLT) station is a 5-minute walk away.
Avoid the hottest hours (12pm–3pm) as it is an open area with no shade.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Line 1 or 2 to Orla Conde station (5 min walk) or Harmonia.
**Bus:** Several lines stop on Rua Sacadura Cabral.
**On foot:** 15 minutes from Praça Mauá.

paco-imperial.name: Paço Imperial (Imperial Palace)
paco-imperial.short_description: The palace where the Portuguese Royal Family lived after 1808. Here Princess Isabel signed the Golden Law in 1888.

content.history:
The history of the Paço Imperial begins in 1603, when the Municipal Council of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro (the city's official name) occupied a simple building by the shore. The present building was constructed between 1738 and 1743, designed by the Portuguese military engineer José Fernandes Pinto Alpoim (1700–1765), by order of governor Gomes Freire de Andrade.

First called the 'Governors' House' and later the 'Governors' Palace', the building gained historical importance in 1763, when the Marquis of Lavradio moved the capital of the Viceroyalty of Brazil from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro. From then on it became the seat of colonial power in Brazil, renamed 'Palace of the Viceroys'.

On 7 March 1808, the Portuguese fleet carrying Dom João VI (then Prince Regent), Queen Dona Maria I and the entire court — about 10,000 to 15,000 people — docked in Rio de Janeiro fleeing Napoleon Bonaparte's troops. The palace was quickly prepared to receive the royal family and came to be called the 'Royal Palace'.

Dom João transformed the palace and Rio de Janeiro: he created the Royal Library (today's National Library), the Botanical Garden, the Royal Press, the Bank of Brazil, and opened Brazilian ports to friendly nations.

On 7 September 1822, Dom Pedro I proclaimed Brazil's Independence. The palace became the 'Imperial Palace'. On 13 May 1888, Princess Imperial Isabel, daughter of Dom Pedro II, signed the Golden Law (Lei Áurea) in this very building, officially abolishing slavery in Brazil.

After the Proclamation of the Republic in 1889, the palace lost its function. It went through decades of neglect until being restored between 1980 and 1985. Today it works as a cultural center with visual-arts exhibitions, shows and cultural events.
curiosities: The Golden Law was signed by Princess Isabel on 13 May 1888 — a date that became the National Day of Abolition.
Dom João VI arrived in Rio with the court in 1808 and turned the city into the capital of the Portuguese Empire — the only time in history a European metropolis was governed from a colony.
The palace was built right by the shore. What is today Praça XV de Novembro was the sea — the landfill was only made in the 20th century.
In 1808, to house the royal family, downtown residents were forced to leave their homes, which were marked 'P.R.' (Príncipe Real) on the doors.
The architect Alpoim also designed the Governors' House in Mariana and the Governors' Palace in Ouro Preto.
The Proclamation of the Republic was on 15 November 1889 — the palace was occupied by troops that day and Dom Pedro II exiled.
practical_tips: Admission is free. Temporary exhibitions may charge.
The inner café of the Paço is great for a break.
Combine it with a visit to Praça XV and the Church of Our Lady of Mount Carmel, right next door.
Exhibitions change frequently — it's worth checking what's on.
It's a 2-minute walk from the Paço Imperial tram (VLT) station.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Paço Imperial station (Line 1) — door to door.
**Subway:** Uruguaiana station (500m walk).
**Bus:** Several lines at Praça XV.

museu-do-amanha.name: Museum of Tomorrow
museu-do-amanha.short_description: A futuristic science museum designed by Santiago Calatrava. One of the most innovative in the world, opened in 2015.
museu-do-amanha.admission: R$ 30 full / R$ 15 half price / Free on Wednesdays

opening_hours.note: Last entry at 5pm

content.history:
The Museum of Tomorrow opened on 17 December 2015, on the eve of the Rio 2016 Olympic Games. It is part of Porto Maravilha, the largest urban public-private partnership in Brazil, which revitalized 5 million m² of Rio's port area.

The building was designed by the Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava, known worldwide for works such as the Milwaukee Art Museum (USA) and the City of Arts and Sciences in Valencia (Spain). The structure extends over Píer Mauá, reaching out over the waters of Guanabara Bay, with 15,000 m² of built area. The project cost approximately R$ 230 million.

The architecture is inspired by the forms of Brazilian nature: the moving 'arms' of the roof resemble Rio's palm trees and can adjust to control sunlight. The museum uses solar energy and water from Guanabara Bay for cooling, and is considered a sustainable building.

The museum does not display traditional objects or collections — it is 100% experiential and interactive. Its content explores humanity's great questions: cosmos, Earth, the Anthropocene (the era of human impact on the planet), tomorrows (possible futures) and the human being.

It was named by the British newspaper The Guardian as one of the 10 best museums opened in 2015 and by Time magazine as one of the most innovative museums in the world.
curiosities: The architect Santiago Calatrava had never designed anything in Brazil before the Museum of Tomorrow.
The roof's moving 'arms' are computer-controlled and move according to the sun's position throughout the day.
The museum was built literally over the sea — the pillars are driven into the bottom of Guanabara Bay.
Water from Guanabara Bay itself circulates through the building's cooling system.
The name 'Tomorrow' was chosen because the museum shows neither the past nor the present — it only questions the future.
The permanent exhibition was developed with the consultancy of 200 scientists from 40 different countries.
practical_tips: Buy tickets online in advance — lines can be long on weekends.
Set aside at least 2 hours for a full visit.
On Wednesdays admission is free for everyone.
The view from the terrace over Guanabara Bay and MAR is a must.
There is a café and restaurant in the museum.
It's a 1-minute walk from the Museum of Tomorrow tram (VLT) station.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Museum of Tomorrow station (Line 1) — direct exit to the museum.
**Bus:** Several lines at Praça Mauá.
**On foot:** 10 minutes from Central do Brasil Station.

mar-museu-arte-rio.name: MAR — Rio Art Museum
mar-museu-arte-rio.short_description: A museum dedicated to the art and history of Rio de Janeiro. Two historic buildings joined by a wavy roof at Praça Mauá.
mar-museu-arte-rio.admission: R$ 20 full / R$ 10 half price / Free on Tuesdays

content.history:
MAR opened on 1 March 2013, becoming the first major cultural facility of the Porto Maravilha project. The museum occupies two historic buildings on Praça Mauá joined by a walkway with a wavy roof evoking the movement of the waters of Guanabara Bay.

One of the buildings is the former Dom João VI Palace, built in 1916, and the other is the former Hotel Império. The integration project was developed by the Rio firm Jacobsen Arquitetura.

The museum focuses on the city of Rio de Janeiro: its history, its art, its conflicts and transformations. The exhibitions explore themes such as the formation of the colonial city, Rio as the imperial capital, the carioca Belle Époque, and the modern and contemporary art produced in the city.
curiosities: The roof joining the two buildings was designed to look like a wave and honor Rio's relationship with the sea.
MAR's terrace offers one of the best views of Guanabara Bay and the Museum of Tomorrow.
The museum receives about 500,000 visitors a year.
The Escola do Olhar (School of Looking), at MAR, offers free visual education for public-school students.
practical_tips: On Tuesdays admission is free.
The terrace is open and free — it's worth going up even if you don't visit the exhibitions.
It's next to the Museum of Tomorrow — you can visit both on the same day.
There is a design shop and a café in the museum.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça Mauá or Museum of Tomorrow station.
**Bus:** Praça Mauá.
**On foot:** 12 minutes from Central do Brasil Station.

aquario-aquario.name: AquaRio
aquario-aquario.short_description: South America's largest aquarium. Opened in 2016 in the port area, with 8,000 animals of 350 species.
aquario-aquario.admission: R$ 100–150 (varies by season) / Half price for students and seniors

content.history:
AquaRio opened on 26 December 2016, becoming the largest aquarium in South America. Located in the Gamboa neighborhood, part of the port area revitalized by Porto Maravilha, the aquarium occupies an area of 8,000 m².

The project was developed by the American firm HKS Architects and is part of the revitalization of Rio's port region for the 2016 Olympic Games. The total investment was approximately R$ 200 million.

AquaRio has 28 tanks with a total capacity of 7 million liters of water — the main tank holds 3.5 million liters. The collection includes more than 8,000 animals of over 350 species, including sharks, rays, octopuses, sea turtles and rare species of tropical fish.
curiosities: The main tank holds 3.5 million liters of water — equivalent to 1,400 Olympic swimming pools.
AquaRio is the largest in South America, surpassing the Buenos Aires Aquarium.
There are tiger sharks at the aquarium — a species that rarely survives in captivity.
The water-filtration process uses cutting-edge Israeli technology.
There is a 7-meter underwater tunnel where visitors walk with fish swimming around them.
practical_tips: Buy tickets in advance on the website — prices may be lower and you avoid lines.
Set aside 2 to 3 hours for the full visit.
Young children love AquaRio — a great family option.
The Santos Dumont tram (VLT) station is a 10-minute walk away.
There is a snack bar and a restaurant on site.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Santos Dumont station (10 min walk).
**Bus:** Rua Sacadura Cabral.
**On foot:** 20 minutes from Praça Mauá.

pedra-do-sal.name: Pedra do Sal
pedra-do-sal.short_description: The cradle of samba in Rio. A historic site of Afro-Brazilian resistance, listed in 1984, with samba circles every week.

opening_hours.Daily: Open 24 hours / Samba: Mon and Fri from 7pm

content.history:
Pedra do Sal is considered the cradle of carioca samba. Located in the Saúde neighborhood — which in the 19th century became known as 'Little Africa' for concentrating Rio's largest free African and Afro-Brazilian population — the Pedra was for centuries a point of Afro-Brazilian cultural resistance.

The name comes from the salt brought from Minas Gerais, which was stored near the rock. The place was a meeting point for African and Afro-Brazilian dockworkers who worked at the port.

In the early decades of the 20th century, founding figures of carioca samba gathered at Pedra do Sal: João da Baiana, Donga (Ernesto dos Santos), Hilário Jovino Ferreira and many others. From here came the first recordings of the musical genre that became a symbol of Brazil.

The Pedra was listed by the city in 1984 as cultural heritage of Rio de Janeiro. Every Monday and Friday night there are traditional samba circles around the rock — one of the most authentic experiences Rio can offer.
curiosities: The first samba recording in history — 'Pelo Telefone' (1917) — is linked to the cultural circle of Pedra do Sal.
The surrounding neighborhood was called 'Little Africa' by the sambista Heitor dos Prazeres in the early 20th century.
João da Baiana, one of the founders of carioca samba, lived and played in the area for decades.
The Pedra is a natural rock formation that existed even before the city was founded.
Listed in 1984 — an act of recognition of Afro-Brazilian culture that preceded the 1988 Constitution.
practical_tips: The samba circles on Mondays and Fridays start around 7pm and go into the early hours.
Bring cash — the drink stalls don't take cards.
It's an open street celebration for everyone — festive and welcoming.
Combine it with a visit to the Valongo Wharf (700m walk).
Largo João da Baiana, where the Pedra is, is easy to find.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Harmonia station (12 min walk).
**Bus:** Rua Sacadura Cabral.
**On foot:** 20 minutes from Praça Mauá.

praca-maua.name: Praça Mauá
praca-maua.short_description: The heart of the port revitalization. Home to the Museum of Tomorrow, MAR and the Olympic Boulevard. It honors the father of Brazilian industry.

content.history:
Praça Mauá honors Irineu Evangelista de Sousa (1813–1889), the Viscount of Mauá — considered the father of industry and modern capitalism in Brazil. The son of a gaúcho farmer, Mauá built Brazil's first shipyard, its first railway (1854), the first transatlantic submarine telegraph cable (1874) and founded the Mauá Bank, which financed the Paraguayan War. He died practically ruined, a victim of conservative resistance to industrial progress.

The square was revitalized as the epicenter of the Porto Maravilha project for the Rio 2016 Olympic Games. The old Perimetral — an elevated viaduct that blocked the view of the port — was demolished in 2013, revealing the waterfront and integrating the square with the port for the first time in decades.

Today Praça Mauá brings together: the Museum of Tomorrow (2015), MAR (2013), tram (VLT) stations, the Olympic Boulevard (with murals by Kobra and other world graffiti artists) and the view of Guanabara Bay with Sugarloaf Mountain in the background.
curiosities: The Viscount of Mauá built Brazil's first railway in 1854 — the Mauá Railroad, linking Petrópolis to the port of Guanabara.
The demolition of the Perimetral viaduct in 2013 opened up the sea view for the first time since the 1960s.
The Olympic Boulevard, opened in 2016, has murals by the artist Eduardo Kobra — the largest is a portrait of five Olympic athletes, 190 meters long.
The square's statue of the Viscount of Mauá was inaugurated in 1943.
The Olympic Boulevard became one of the city's main open-air street-art galleries.
practical_tips: The best view of the square is at night, when the Museum of Tomorrow is lit up.
The Olympic Boulevard has several world-class graffiti murals — worth the walk.
There are kiosks and restaurants along the boulevard.
It's a starting point for boat trips around Guanabara Bay.
It's between the Praça Mauá and Museum of Tomorrow tram (VLT) stations.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça Mauá or Museum of Tomorrow stations.
**Bus:** Several lines.
**On foot:** 10 minutes from Central do Brasil Station.

real-gabinete-portugues-leitura.name: Royal Portuguese Reading Room
real-gabinete-portugues-leitura.short_description: A library founded in 1837 by Portuguese immigrants. Considered one of the most beautiful in the world by National Geographic.

content.history:
The Royal Portuguese Reading Room (Real Gabinete Português de Leitura) was founded in 1837 by a group of Portuguese immigrants living in Rio de Janeiro, led by merchants and intellectuals who wanted to keep Portuguese culture and language alive in Brazil.

The present building, a jewel of Manueline architecture (the 16th-century Portuguese Gothic style), was inaugurated on 30 October 1887. It was designed by the Portuguese architect Rafael da Silva e Castro, who drew inspiration from the Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon. The lioz-stone façade, brought from Portugal, is richly ornamented with royal shields, armillary spheres and maritime elements evoking the Age of Discovery.

The library holds more than 350,000 volumes, including rare works from the 16th, 17th and 18th centuries — it is one of the largest collections in the Portuguese language outside Portugal. Among the treasures are first editions of Camões's The Lusiads, works by Eça de Queirós, Almeida Garrett and historical manuscripts.

The central reading room — with its cast-iron structure, glass ceiling and shelves rising to the ceiling — was named by National Geographic one of the 10 most beautiful libraries in the world.
curiosities: National Geographic included the Royal Reading Room in its list of the 10 most beautiful libraries in the world.
The architectural style is Manueline — the same as the Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon, built to celebrate Vasco da Gama's voyages.
The façade stone is Portuguese lioz — the same marble used in Lisbon's pavements.
Among the rare books are original editions of Camões's The Lusiads, the greatest epic poem in the Portuguese language.
The library has operated since 1837 — surviving the Empire, the Republic, wars and pandemics without closing.
The central chandelier of the reading room is over 100 years old and was brought from Portugal.
practical_tips: The visit is completely free — just walk in.
Take a photo of the central room: it's stunning.
The collection can be consulted on site — it is an active library.
It's in the heart of the historic Downtown, 5 minutes from Praça XV and the Lapa Arches.
Silence is required — it is a place of study and reading.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway:** Carioca (200m walk) or Uruguaiana (300m walk).
**Tram (VLT):** Carioca station.
**Bus:** Several lines on Rua Uruguaiana.

arcos-da-lapa.name: Lapa Arches (Arcos da Lapa)
arcos-da-lapa.short_description: A colonial aqueduct from 1750 with 42 arches. Today it carries the Santa Teresa tram and is a symbol of bohemian Rio.
arcos-da-lapa.address: Rua Joaquim Silva / Largo da Lapa — Lapa

content.history:
The Lapa Arches — officially the Carioca Aqueduct — were built between 1723 and 1750 to carry water from the Carioca River (now covered beneath the city) to the fountain at Largo do Paço (today's Praça XV), supplying the growing population of colonial Rio de Janeiro.

The structure is 270 meters long and 17.6 meters high, supported by 42 arches of stone and lime. It was one of the great engineering feats of colonial Rio, supplying the city for almost 150 years.

In 1896 the aqueduct lost its original function and was adapted into a viaduct for the electric tram line connecting Downtown to the Santa Teresa neighborhood — a function it still serves today. The famous Santa Teresa tram still crosses the Lapa Arches daily, one of the last tram systems still operating in Brazil.

Over the 20th century, the area around the Arches became the heart of carioca bohemia. Lapa is famous for its bars, its samba circles and the historic presence of artists, musicians and intellectuals. It was the stage for figures such as Madame Satã (João Francisco dos Santos) and inspired works by Nelson Rodrigues and Noel Rosa.
curiosities: The aqueduct used dressed stone brought from Portugal as ship ballast — the same stone that paves parts of the historic Downtown.
For decades Lapa was an area of prostitution and marginality — today it is an internationally recognized tourist and samba hotspot.
The Santa Teresa tram, which crosses the Arches, has existed since 1877 — one of the oldest transport systems still operating in Brazil.
In 2011 the Santa Teresa tram suffered a serious accident; service was suspended and only resumed in 2015 after modernization.
Madame Satã, one of Lapa's most famous figures, lived in the neighborhood from 1900 to 1976, known for his irreverence and bravery.
practical_tips: At night (Thursday to Saturday) Lapa buzzes with samba circles, shows and bars — a unique atmosphere in Rio.
Be careful at night: Lapa is lively but requires attention to your belongings.
The Santa Teresa tram departs from the nearby square — the ride up to the historic neighborhood is worth it.
Combine the Lapa Arches with the Selarón Steps, a 3-minute walk away.
There are excellent restaurants near the Arches: Carioca da Gema and Rio Scenarium are the most famous.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway:** Cinelândia station (700m walk).
**Bus:** Several lines on Avenida Mem de Sá.
**On foot:** 15 minutes from the Paço Imperial.

escadaria-selaron.name: Selarón Steps
escadaria-selaron.short_description: 215 steps covered in tiles from 60 countries. A work by the artist Jorge Selarón, a symbol of Rio's cultural diversity.
escadaria-selaron.address: Rua Joaquim Silva — Lapa / Santa Teresa

content.history:
The Selarón Steps are one of the most famous works of urban art in the world. Created by the artist Jorge Selarón (1947–2013), born in Chile but settled in Rio de Janeiro, the staircase connects the Lapa and Santa Teresa neighborhoods along 215 steps.

Selarón began covering the steps with tiles in 1990, after moving into a house on Rua Joaquim Silva with the deteriorated staircase in front of it. At first he used scraps and broken tiles found in the streets. Over time the project gained worldwide fame and people from all over the planet started sending tiles from their countries — today the staircase has pieces from more than 60 nations.

Selarón said the work was 'my tribute to the Brazilian people'. He obsessively repeated the image of a pregnant Black woman on different tiles — according to him, a homage to motherhood and the Afro-Brazilian people.

On 10 January 2013 — his 65th birthday — Jorge Selarón was found dead on the staircase, in circumstances that were never fully clarified. His death caused worldwide commotion. The staircase was listed as cultural heritage of Rio de Janeiro.

The Selarón Steps have appeared in videos by artists such as Snoop Dogg and Pharrell Williams, in 2014 World Cup ads, and in countless films and documentaries about Rio.
curiosities: Selarón worked on the staircase for more than 20 years, every day, until his death.
The most repeated image on the staircase is a pregnant Black woman — according to the artist, 'the mother of Brazil'.
The tiles came from more than 60 countries, sent by tourists, admirers and fans worldwide.
Selarón never sold the staircase or accepted sponsorships — it was a personal, non-commercial work.
The staircase appears in Snoop Dogg's 'Beautiful' video, becoming world-famous in the early 2000s.
After Selarón's death, the maintenance and restoration of the tiles became the responsibility of the Rio city government.
practical_tips: The staircase lies between Lapa and Santa Teresa — you can go down toward Lapa or up toward Santa Teresa.
Best time for photos: early morning (before 9am) or late afternoon (after 5pm) — fewer tourists and better light.
It gets very crowded on weekends — weekdays are quieter.
There are street vendors nearby with souvenirs.
It's a 3-minute walk from the Lapa Arches.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway:** Cinelândia (10 min walk) or Glória (12 min walk).
**On foot:** 5 minutes from the Lapa Arches.

confeitaria-colombo.name: Confeitaria Colombo
confeitaria-colombo.short_description: Founded in 1894, one of Brazil's greatest gastronomic landmarks. Art Nouveau interior with Belgian mirrors and jacaranda wood.
confeitaria-colombo.admission: Free entry (pay for what you order)

content.history:
Confeitaria Colombo was founded on 17 November 1894 by two Portuguese immigrants, Joaquim Borges and Manuel José Lebrão. The name is a tribute to Christopher Columbus.

The Art Nouveau interior we see today was remodeled in 1913 by the Portuguese Porto brothers, architects. The enormous Belgian crystal mirrors — brought directly from Belgium — create the effect of an endless hall. The woodwork is solid jacaranda, and the ceiling has colored stained glass that softly filters the sunlight.

During the carioca Belle Époque (1900–1930), the Colombo was the meeting point of Rio's intellectual and political elite. Writers such as Olavo Bilac, Lima Barreto and Machado de Assis frequented the hall. Politicians debated there while tasting the famous cakes and imported cigars. President Getúlio Vargas was a regular customer.

The Colombo has survived 130 years of Brazilian history: the end of the Empire, the Old Republic, the Vargas Era, the military dictatorship and the return to democracy. It became cultural heritage of Rio de Janeiro.
curiosities: The mirrors were brought from Belgium in 1913 — around the same period the Titanic was crossing the Atlantic.
Machado de Assis, considered the greatest Brazilian writer, frequented the Colombo regularly.
The 1913 remodel cost a fortune — the solid jacaranda wood would be impossible to reproduce legally today.
The Colombo survived intact the demolitions of the Pereira Passos reform (1900–1910), which destroyed much of the historic center.
In 2024 the Colombo celebrated 130 years — one of the oldest patisseries in the world still operating in the same location.
practical_tips: Don't miss the dulce de leche mille-feuille — the house specialty — or the traditional afternoon tea.
A few minutes from Praça Tiradentes and the historic center.
The high tea is famous and reserved — it's worth calling ahead.
The cakes and sweets are among the best in the historic center.
Lines can be long on weekends — arrive early.
Combine it with a visit to the Royal Portuguese Reading Room (5-minute walk).
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway:** Carioca (3 min walk) or Uruguaiana (5 min walk).
**Tram (VLT):** Carioca station.
**Bus:** Several lines downtown.

ccbb-rio.name: Banco do Brasil Cultural Center (CCBB)
ccbb-rio.short_description: One of the most visited cultural centers in Brazil — exhibitions, cinema, theater and music, in a 1906 palace.
ccbb-rio.admission: Free entry (some exhibitions may charge — check the website)

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: The **CCBB Rio** occupies an elegant building opened in 1906 that was the headquarters of Banco do Brasil, with an eclectic/neoclassical façade and a central rotunda topped by a skylight. The space was converted into a cultural center and opened in **October 1989**. It became one of the most visited cultural centers in Brazil and Latin America, with an intense program of art exhibitions, cinema, theater, music and educational activities. It sits in the heart of the historic-financial circuit of Downtown, near the Candelária Church and the Casa França-Brasil.
curiosities: The building was Banco do Brasil's headquarters before becoming a cultural center in 1989.
In years of major exhibitions it is often among the most visited cultural centers in the world.
practical_tips: Admission is usually free, but popular exhibitions require picking up a ticket (often through the website).
Hours and programming change — confirm on the official website before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Candelária or Sete de Setembro station (a few minutes' walk).
**Subway:** Uruguaiana station.
On Rua Primeiro de Março, downtown.

theatro-municipal-rio.name: Municipal Theater of Rio de Janeiro
theatro-municipal-rio.short_description: Rio's main opera and ballet house, opened in 1909 and inspired by the Paris Opera.
theatro-municipal-rio.admission: Guided tours and performances — check prices on the official website

opening_hours.note: Guided tours and performances have their own schedules — check the official website

content.history: Opened on **14 July 1909**, the **Municipal Theater** is one of the most imposing performance houses in Brazil. Its design is inspired by the Paris Opera (Palais Garnier), with a monumental façade, marble staircases, stained glass and a grand hall. It is part of the Cinelândia ensemble (Praça Floriano), alongside the National Library and the National Museum of Fine Arts. It hosts seasons of opera, ballet and symphonic concerts and is considered the country's leading lyric stage.
curiosities: The hall and the grand areas feature panels by the painter Eliseu Visconti.
Together with the National Library and the MNBA, it forms the monumental ensemble of Cinelândia.
practical_tips: There are guided tours of the interior at specific times — confirm on the website.
For performances, buy tickets in advance.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway/Tram (VLT):** Cinelândia station, right in front.
On Praça Floriano, downtown.

mosteiro-de-sao-bento.name: Monastery of São Bento
mosteiro-de-sao-bento.short_description: A Benedictine monastery from 1590 with one of the richest Baroque churches in Brazil.

opening_hours.note: Visiting and mass times may change — confirm before going

content.history: Founded in **1590** by Benedictine monks on the São Bento Hill, the monastery houses the **Church of Our Lady of Monserrate**, one of the most important Baroque ensembles in Brazil. Construction of the church advanced through the 17th and 18th centuries, and its interior is almost entirely covered in **gilded woodwork**, with richly ornamented altars, chapels and ceilings. It remains a working monastery as well as a prominent tourist and religious site downtown.
curiosities: The interior is covered in Baroque gilded woodwork — one of the richest in the country.
On Sundays there is usually a mass with Gregorian chant.
practical_tips: Keep silence and wear appropriate clothing — it is an active place of worship.
Confirm visiting and mass times before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** São Bento station.
Access via Rua Dom Gerardo (there is an elevator at no. 40), downtown.

igreja-da-candelaria.name: Candelária Church
igreja-da-candelaria.short_description: An imposing downtown church, built between 1775 and 1898, a historic landmark of Rio.

opening_hours.note: Visiting and mass times may change — confirm before going

content.history: The **Church of Our Lady of Candelária** is one of the most imposing in Rio. The present church was built between **1775 and 1898**, combining Baroque and Neoclassical elements, with a large dome, a façade of Portuguese lioz stone and an interior clad in marble. It stands on Praça Pio X, a landmark of the Downtown landscape, between Avenida Presidente Vargas and Rua Primeiro de Março.
curiosities: According to tradition, the name comes from the ship Candelária, whose survivors of a storm built the first chapel on the site in the 17th century.
The bronze doors and interior marbles were brought from Europe.
practical_tips: It's a few steps from the CCBB and the Casa França-Brasil — you can combine the visits.
Confirm visiting and mass times on site.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Candelária station (right in front).
On Praça Pio X, downtown.

biblioteca-nacional.name: National Library
biblioteca-nacional.short_description: The largest library in Latin America, with a collection started by the royal family in 1810.

opening_hours.note: Visiting and consultation hours may change — confirm on the official website

content.history: The **National Library** is the largest in Latin America and one of the largest in the world. Its origin goes back to the **Royal Library** brought from Portugal by Dom João VI in **1810**, when the court moved to Rio fleeing Napoleon's troops. The present building, on Avenida Rio Branco, was inaugurated in **1910**, in an eclectic style, forming part of the monumental Cinelândia ensemble. It holds millions of items, including rare books, manuscripts, maps, periodicals and music scores.
curiosities: The collection grew from the Royal Library brought by Dom João VI in 1810.
It holds rare works and is recognized by UNESCO under the Memory of the World Programme.
practical_tips: There are free guided tours at set times — confirm on the website.
Bring ID to access the consultation areas.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway/Tram (VLT):** Cinelândia station (right in front).
On Avenida Rio Branco, downtown.

igreja-nossa-senhora-carmo.name: Church of Our Lady of Mount Carmel (Old Cathedral)
igreja-nossa-senhora-carmo.short_description: A 16th-century church that was the Cathedral of Imperial Brazil. Dom Pedro I and Dom Pedro II were baptized here.

content.history:
The Church of Our Lady of Mount Carmel of the Old See (Antiga Sé) has origins in the 16th century. The Carmelite Convent was founded by Carmelite priests in 1590, one of the first permanent religious buildings in colonial Rio de Janeiro.

When Dom João VI arrived in Rio de Janeiro in 1808 fleeing Napoleon, the convent was given to the royal family. The complex was adapted as the Royal Palace — the convent became the court's residence and the Carmo Church began to serve as the Royal Chapel. From 1808, the Carmo Church became the Cathedral of Brazil, a role it held until 1976, when the new Metropolitan Cathedral (the cone-shaped one) was inaugurated.

The church's historical importance is immeasurable: Dom Pedro I and Dom Pedro II were baptized here. The Portuguese royal family attended mass here. Brazil's independence was celebrated with a Te Deum in this temple.

The Baroque interior is dazzling: gilding, wood carvings, ceiling paintings and altars richly decorated with gold. The patron saint, Our Lady of Mount Carmel, is one of the oldest religious images in Rio.
curiosities: Dom Pedro I (who proclaimed Brazil's Independence) and Dom Pedro II (who abolished slavery) were both baptized in this church.
When the Royal Family arrived in 1808, the Carmelite priests were expelled from the convent to make room for the Portuguese court.
The first mass after the Royal Family's arrival was celebrated in this very church, on 8 March 1808.
The gold covering the church's interior came from Minas Gerais in the 18th century — Rio de Janeiro was the export port for colonial gold.
The word 'Sé' comes from the Latin 'sedes' (seat) — indicating the church was the seat of the bishopric, i.e. the main cathedral.
practical_tips: The visit is free and the interior is very well preserved.
It's a 2-minute walk from the Paço Imperial and Praça XV.
Sunday masses have a choir — worth attending for the musical experience.
The Paço Imperial + Carmo Church + Praça XV combination is the richest historic core of Rio.
Silence and appropriate clothing are requested.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Paço Imperial station.
**Subway:** Uruguaiana (600m walk).
**On foot:** Next to the Paço Imperial, at Praça XV.

casa-franca-brasil.name: Casa França-Brasil
casa-franca-brasil.short_description: An 1820 neoclassical building by Grandjean de Montigny, today a cultural center.

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: **Casa França-Brasil** occupies one of the first neoclassical buildings in Brazil, designed by **Grandjean de Montigny**, architect of the French Artistic Mission, and inaugurated in **1820**. It was conceived as Rio's first **Commercial Exchange (Praça do Comércio)** and had various uses over time (customs house, court, archive). Restored, it became a cultural center in **1990**, with contemporary art programming and exhibitions. It is on Rua Visconde de Itaboraí, next to the CCBB and the former Post Office building.
curiosities: It was Rio de Janeiro's first Commercial Exchange (Praça do Comércio).
It is the work of Grandjean de Montigny, of the 1816 French Artistic Mission.
practical_tips: It's on the same cultural block as the CCBB and the Correios Cultural Center.
Free entry — confirm the program on the official website.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Candelária station (a few minutes' walk).
On Rua Visconde de Itaboraí, downtown.

centro-cultural-correios.name: Correios Cultural Center
centro-cultural-correios.short_description: The postal service's cultural center in a historic downtown building, with exhibitions and theater.

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: The **Correios Cultural Center** occupies a historic building from the early 20th century, on Rua Visconde de Itaboraí, in the heart of Downtown's cultural circuit — next to the CCBB and the Casa França-Brasil. Converted into a cultural center in the 1990s, it offers exhibitions, theater and activities, and is known for its Christmas decorations. The building preserves architectural elements of the carioca Belle Époque.
curiosities: It is part of the Downtown cultural block, alongside the CCBB and the Casa França-Brasil.
It is traditionally known for its end-of-year decorations and programming.
practical_tips: Combine the visit with the CCBB and the Casa França-Brasil, on the same block.
Free entry — check the program on the website.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Candelária station (a few minutes' walk).
On Rua Visconde de Itaboraí, downtown.

catedral-metropolitana-rio.name: Metropolitan Cathedral of São Sebastião
catedral-metropolitana-rio.short_description: A 1979 modernist cathedral, cone-shaped, with stained-glass windows rising from floor to top.

opening_hours.note: Visiting and mass times may change — confirm before going

content.history: The **Metropolitan Cathedral of São Sebastião** is the cathedral of Rio de Janeiro, inaugurated in **1979**. Modernist in architecture, it has a conical shape (recalling Mayan pyramids), is about **96 meters tall**, and has four large rectilinear stained-glass windows rising from floor to top, creating a monumental interior lit by colored light. It holds thousands of worshippers and is on Avenida República do Chile, near Carioca and the Lapa Arches.
curiosities: It is about 96 meters tall, with four immense stained-glass windows from floor to top.
Its conical shape is inspired by pre-Columbian pyramids.
practical_tips: It's a few minutes from Carioca station and the Lapa Arches.
Confirm visiting and mass times on site.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway/Tram (VLT):** Carioca station (2 min walk).
On Avenida República do Chile, downtown.

igreja-sao-francisco-de-paula.name: Church of São Francisco de Paula
igreja-sao-francisco-de-paula.short_description: An 18th-century colonial church at Largo de São Francisco de Paula, next to the SAARA market.
igreja-sao-francisco-de-paula.address: Largo de São Francisco de Paula — Downtown

opening_hours.note: Visiting and mass times may change — confirm before going

content.history: The **Church of São Francisco de Paula** began construction in **1759** and was completed over the course of the 19th century. It is an important example of carioca Baroque and Rococo, with rich interior ornamentation that includes works attributed to the sculptor **Mestre Valentim** and paintings by Brazilian artists. It is at Largo de São Francisco de Paula, next to the popular SAARA shopping area, in the historic Downtown.
curiosities: It holds works attributed to the sculptor Mestre Valentim in its interior.
It sits next to SAARA, the largest popular shopping hub in Downtown.
practical_tips: Combine it with a walk through SAARA and Praça Tiradentes.
Confirm visiting and mass times on site.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Saara or Carioca station.
At Largo de São Francisco de Paula, downtown.

museu-historico-nacional.name: National Historical Museum
museu-historico-nacional.short_description: Brazil's largest history museum, founded in 1922 in colonial fortifications.
museu-historico-nacional.admission: Check prices on the official website (free on specific days)

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: Founded in **1922**, on the centenary of Independence, the **National Historical Museum** occupies a set of buildings that includes the former **Casa do Trem** (1762) and the **Santiago Fort**, by the waterfront, near Praça XV. It holds the largest history collection in Brazil — about 300,000 items, including coins (the largest numismatic collection in Latin America), weapons, furniture, carriages, works of art and objects from the colonial, imperial and republican periods.
curiosities: It occupies 18th-century fortifications, such as the Casa do Trem and the Santiago Fort.
It holds one of the largest numismatic (coin) collections in Latin America.
practical_tips: It's near Praça XV and the Paço Imperial — good for combining visits.
Confirm hours and prices on the official website.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Antônio Carlos–MAM or Cinelândia station.
Near Praça XV, downtown.

museu-nacional-belas-artes.name: National Museum of Fine Arts (MNBA)
museu-nacional-belas-artes.short_description: The country's largest visual-arts collection, with a focus on 19th- and 20th-century Brazilian art.
museu-nacional-belas-artes.admission: Check prices on the official website (free on specific days)

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: The **National Museum of Fine Arts** opened in **1938**, housed in the 1908 building designed by Adolfo Morales de los Ríos on Avenida Rio Branco, in Cinelândia. It holds the largest visual-arts collection in Brazil — more than 20,000 pieces — with strong representation of **19th- and 20th-century Brazilian art**, including works by Victor Meirelles, Pedro Américo, Almeida Júnior and Rodolfo Amoedo, as well as foreign and African art.
curiosities: It holds masterpieces such as canvases by Victor Meirelles and Pedro Américo.
The building is part of the monumental Cinelândia ensemble, next to the Municipal Theater.
practical_tips: It's across from the Cinelândia subway/tram (VLT) — easy access.
Confirm hours and prices on the official website.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway/Tram (VLT):** Cinelândia station (right in front).
On Avenida Rio Branco, downtown.

academia-brasileira-de-letras.name: Brazilian Academy of Letters
academia-brasileira-de-letras.short_description: Home of the 'immortals' of Brazilian literature, founded in 1897, in the Petit Trianon.
academia-brasileira-de-letras.admission: Free entry (guided tours at set times)

opening_hours.note: Visits at set times — confirm on the official website

content.history: The **Brazilian Academy of Letters (ABL)** was founded in **1897**, with **Machado de Assis** as its first president. It brings together 40 members — the so-called 'immortals' — each occupying a chair with a patron. Its headquarters is the **Petit Trianon**, a replica of the Petit Trianon of Versailles, donated by France and inaugurated in 1923, on Avenida Presidente Wilson, near the Museum of Modern Art and Santos Dumont airport.
curiosities: Machado de Assis was the Academy's first president.
The headquarters is the Petit Trianon, a gift from France — a replica of the Versailles pavilion.
practical_tips: There are guided tours at specific times — confirm on the ABL website.
It's near the MAM and Santos Dumont airport.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway/Tram (VLT):** Cinelândia station.
On Avenida Presidente Wilson, near the MAM, downtown.

palacio-tiradentes.name: Tiradentes Palace
palacio-tiradentes.short_description: Historic seat of Rio's State Assembly (ALERJ) by Praça XV. An eclectic 1926 palace built where the old jail that held Tiradentes once stood. It has a museum and a free guided tour.
palacio-tiradentes.address: Rua Primeiro de Março, s/n — Downtown (Praça XV)

opening_hours.note: Guided tours at set times — confirm on the official website

content.history: **Tiradentes Palace**, seat of the **Legislative Assembly of the State of Rio (ALERJ)**, is by Praça XV, downtown. The **present eclectic building was inaugurated in 1926** (designed by Archimedes Memória and Francisque Couchet). On the same site stood the old **Cadeia Velha (Old Jail)**, where **Tiradentes was imprisoned** before being executed in 1792 — hence the name. It houses a permanent exhibition on the history of the Brazilian parliament, open to visitors.
curiosities: It is named after Tiradentes, who was held in the old jail that stood on the site.
The present building, from 1926, is in the eclectic style.
It was once the seat of the Chamber of Deputies (1926–1960).
It has a permanent exhibition on parliament and a free guided tour.
practical_tips: The guided tours have set times — confirm on the official website.
It's next to the Paço Imperial and Praça XV.
Free entry.
Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station (Lines 2 and 4).
On Rua Primeiro de Março, by Praça XV, downtown.

praca-xv.name: Praça XV de Novembro
praca-xv.short_description: The historic heart of Rio: the former Terreiro do Paço, with the Paço Imperial, Tiradentes Palace, the Teles Arch and the Mestre Valentim Fountain (1789). Ferry terminal to Niterói.

content.history: **Praça XV de Novembro** (Praça Quinze) is the historic heart of Rio: it was the former **Terreiro do Paço**, the center of colonial and imperial power. It brings together the **Paço Imperial**, **Tiradentes Palace**, the **Teles Arch** and the **Mestre Valentim Fountain** (1789). The name recalls the Proclamation of the Republic (15 November 1889). The **ferries to Niterói** depart from here.
curiosities: It was the Terreiro do Paço, the center of colonial and imperial Rio.
It has the Mestre Valentim Fountain, from 1789.
It brings together the Paço Imperial, Tiradentes Palace and the Teles Arch.
It is the ferry terminal to Niterói.
practical_tips: Combine it with the Paço Imperial and Tiradentes Palace.
The ferries to Niterói depart from here, with a beautiful bay view.
Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praça XV station (Lines 2 and 4).
Downtown, on the shore of Guanabara Bay.

angu-do-gomes.name: Angu do Gomes
angu-do-gomes.short_description: A port-workers' classic since 1955. Creamy angu with sarapatel, oxtail, meat or seafood, at Largo da Prainha.
angu-do-gomes.address: Rua Sacadura Cabral, 75 (Largo da Prainha) — Saúde

opening_hours.note: Sat/Sun: please confirm

content.history: **Angu do Gomes** was born in **1955**, from João Gomes's pushcarts that spread angu with offal across Downtown Rio. In 1977 it got a permanent home at **Largo de São Francisco da Prainha**, in Saúde, and became a symbol of carioca street food.
curiosities: Creamy angu with sarapatel, oxtail, meat or seafood.
Open since 1955 — it started with pushcarts around Downtown.
It's at Largo de São Francisco da Prainha, in Saúde.
practical_tips: Tip: order the classic angu with a fried egg.
Ideal for a quick, cheap lunch.
Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:

At Rua Sacadura Cabral, 75 (Largo da Prainha), Saúde.

dois-de-fevereiro.name: Dois de Fevereiro
dois-de-fevereiro.short_description: Bahian and Little Africa cuisine by chef João Diamante — shrimp bobó, moqueca and seafood, at Largo da Prainha.

opening_hours.note: Confirm before going

content.history: **Dois de Fevereiro** is at Largo de São Francisco da Prainha, in Saúde, and pays homage to Iemanjá. Its kitchen is led by the award-winning chef **João Diamante**, who celebrates his Bahian roots and the culture of Little Africa.
curiosities: Bahian dishes: shrimp bobó, moqueca and seafood.
Led by award-winning chef João Diamante.
Named one of the city's best Brazilian restaurants in 2025 (Rio Show Gastronomy awards).

Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.

At Rua Sacadura Cabral, 79, Saúde.

gg-gourmet.name: G&G Gourmet
gg-gourmet.short_description: Little Africa's famous shrimp bobó and regional snacks, on Rua Sacadura Cabral.

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm before going

content.history: **G&G Gourmet** is on Rua Sacadura Cabral, in Saúde, within Little Africa. It is known for its shrimp bobó and its Afro-Brazilian heritage cuisine.
curiosities: Shrimp bobó is the flagship dish.
In the heart of Little Africa.
Regional snacks.

cinelandia-praca-floriano.name: Cinelândia — Praça Floriano
cinelandia-praca-floriano.short_description: Rio's cultural and historic center. The Municipal Theater, National Library and National Museum of Fine Arts stand on this iconic square.

content.history:
Praça Floriano — known as Cinelândia — is the cultural heart of Rio de Janeiro. Named after Marshal Floriano Peixoto (1839–1895), Brazil's second president, the square earned the nickname 'Cinelândia' in the 1920s, when several luxury cinemas opened around it: Cine Pathé, Cine Glória, Cine Odeon, Cine Ideal — all now demolished or transformed.

Around the square stand four monuments of carioca eclectic architecture:

**Municipal Theater (1909):** Opened on 14 July 1909 (the anniversary of the French Revolution, a date deliberately chosen by Mayor Pereira Passos), inspired by the Paris Opera (Palais Garnier). Designed by Francisco de Oliveira Passos and Albert Guilbert. With 2,357 seats, it is one of the largest and most beautiful theaters in Latin America, with a permanent program of opera, ballet and concerts.

**National Library (1910):** Founded in 1810 by Dom João VI, who brought 60,000 volumes from the Royal Library of Lisbon when he fled Napoleon. The present building opened in 1910, designed by Francisco Marcelino de Souza Aguiar. With more than 9 million items, it is the largest library in Latin America.

**National Museum of Fine Arts (1938):** Housed in the same building as the former National School of Fine Arts, created by Dom João VI in 1816. Designed by Adolfo Morales de los Rios, opened in 1908 as a school and in 1938 as a museum. Its collection includes works by Víctor Meirelles, Pedro Américo and 19th-century European painters.

**City Council (1923):** Seat of the carioca legislature, in neoclassical style.
curiosities: The Municipal Theater opened on 14 July — a date chosen to honor the French Revolution.
Dom João VI founded the National Library in 1810 with books brought from Lisbon while fleeing Napoleon — 60,000 volumes crossed the Atlantic.
The Cine Odeon, one of Cinelândia's last cinemas, was restored and still operates — today as an art-house cinema.
Cinelândia was Rio's biggest entertainment hub in the 1920s–1950s, with more than 10 cinemas on the same square.
Floriano Peixoto, who gives the square its name, was known as 'the Iron Marshal' for harshly suppressing the Naval Revolt and the Federalist Revolution in the 1890s.
The Municipal Theater has a secret ballroom in the basement that was used for carioca elite parties in the early 20th century.
practical_tips: Visit the Municipal Theater: guides offer free tours on Saturday mornings.
The National Library also offers free guided tours — check times on the website.
The National Museum of Fine Arts has free admission on Saturdays.
On Thursday and Friday nights, Cinelândia's bars fill up — a lively atmosphere.
The Cine Odeon still shows films — check the schedule.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Subway:** Cinelândia (direct exit to the square).
**Bus:** Several lines on Av. Rio Branco.

It emerged in the 17th century and was once called **Rossio Grande**, **Campo dos Ciganos** (Gypsies' Field), **Campo da Lampadosa** and **Campo do Polé**. It took its current name in **1890**, in honor of Tiradentes, who was executed nearby in 1792. At its center stands the **equestrian monument to Dom Pedro I** (1862, by the French sculptor Louis Rochet) — often mistaken for a statue of Tiradentes.
curiosities: It has had several names: Rossio Grande, Campo dos Ciganos, Campo da Lampadosa and Campo do Polé.
The central monument is of Dom Pedro I (1862), not Tiradentes.

praca-da-harmonia.name: Praça da Harmonia

content.history: The **Praça da Harmonia**, officially **Praça Coronel Assunção**, is in **Gamboa**, in the port area. The site once held the **Harmonia Market**, which deteriorated over time; after the 1900 bubonic-plague epidemic and episodes linked to the **Vaccine Revolt** (1904), the area was redeveloped under Mayor **Pereira Passos**, giving rise to the present square. The name honors **Colonel Assunção** (Joaquim Antonio Fernandes Assunção, born 1823), an officer who fought in the Paraguayan War and commanded the Court's police; a bust in his honor, by the sculptor Ruffo Fanucchi, was unveiled in 1959. The square is also remembered as the **founding place of Club de Regatas Vasco da Gama, on 21 August 1898**.

cemiterio-dos-ingleses.short_description: Brazil's oldest open-air cemetery still in use, in Gamboa. Created in the early 19th c. for British Protestants, after the Royal Family's arrival and the opening of the ports.

content.history: The **English Cemetery** (British Burial Ground), in **Gamboa**, is the **oldest open-air cemetery in Brazil still in operation**. It emerged in the early 19th century: with the arrival of the **Royal Family (1808)** and the **opening of the ports**, the British presence grew — mostly Anglicans and Protestants — who were barred from burial in Catholic churches. Under the **Treaty of Friendship and Commerce (1810)** between Portugal and England, the land was granted and the **first burial took place on 15 January 1811**. It even had its own wharf on the old Saco da Gamboa inlet.

central-do-brasil.name: Central do Brasil Station
central-do-brasil.short_description: The largest railway station in South America. A 135m tower, opened in 1943. The starting point of Rio's commuter trains.
central-do-brasil.admission: Free (concourse access) / Ticket for the train

content.history:
The history of Central do Brasil Station began in 1858, when construction of the Dom Pedro II Railway started — Rio de Janeiro's first railway and one of Brazil's first. The original station was much simpler and stood on the same site.

The present building opened on 1 September 1943, under President Getúlio Vargas, in Art Deco style. It was designed by architects Mario Russo and others. The clock tower is 135 meters tall and was, for decades, one of the tallest structures in Rio de Janeiro. The clock has kept time ever since.

'A Central', as cariocas call it, is the largest railway hub in Brazil and one of the busiest in South America. Hundreds of thousands of people pass through it daily on the SuperVia (commuter trains) and subway lines.

In 1998, Walter Salles's film 'Central Station' turned the station into a worldwide cinematic symbol. The film — about a woman who writes letters for illiterate people at the station — won the Golden Bear at the Berlin Festival and was nominated for the Oscar for Best Foreign Film. Actress Fernanda Montenegro received an Oscar nomination for Best Actress.
curiosities: The film 'Central Station' (1998) was nominated for an Oscar and won the Golden Bear in Berlin — putting the station on the world's cultural map.
The clock tower is 135 meters tall — one of the highest points in Rio when it was built.
More than 600,000 people pass through Central each day — one of the busiest stations in South America.
On the square out front there are always medicine sellers, prayer-women and onlookers — Praça Cristiano Ottoni concentrates Rio's most popular and diverse side.
The Dom Pedro II Railway, opened in 1858, pioneered the use of steam locomotives in Brazil.
The station has a secret (deactivated) platform that was used to transport authorities during the 20th century.
practical_tips: It's an active station — watch your belongings at peak times.
The main concourse can be visited for free.
Trains depart from here to all of Rio's North Zone and lowlands.
It's an interchange with the subway (Lines 1 and 2) and the tram (VLT).
The Praça Cristiano Ottoni outside is lively — typical of working-class Rio.

morro-da-conceicao.name: Morro da Conceição
morro-da-conceicao.short_description: A colonial enclave in the heart of the port area. Cobblestone streets, 18th-century townhouses and the Conceição Fort.
morro-da-conceicao.address: Rua Bela / Rua Monte Alegre — Saúde

content.history:
The Morro da Conceição is one of the seven original hills on which Rio de Janeiro was founded. It is one of the rare parts of the city where the colonial layout is still preserved: narrow cobblestone streets, alleys, 18th- and 19th-century townhouses and silence — a surreal contrast with the bustle of the surrounding port.

The Conceição Fort was built between 1710 and 1713, during the period of the wars against the French and the conflict over the Sacramento Colony. It served as a political prison in different periods of Brazilian history.

The Casa do Trem (1762) is one of the oldest buildings still standing in Rio — originally a military-supplies depot, today part of the Army Historical Museum.

In the 19th century, the Morro was inhabited by the carioca elite. In the 20th century, with the decline of the port area, the neighborhood was forgotten — which paradoxically preserved its historic character. Today it is a quiet residential neighborhood with longtime residents and some artists and intellectuals who chose the area for its silence and history.
curiosities: The Morro da Conceição is called 'the carioca Montmartre' — a comparison with the historic artists' neighborhood in Paris.
During the Vaccine Revolt (1904), the neighborhood was the scene of clashes between residents and the police enforcing compulsory vaccination.
There is a viewpoint at the top of the hill with a panoramic view of Guanabara Bay and the port area.
The street stones are original from the 18th century — they have never been replaced.
The Instituto Moreira Salles has a cultural space on the Morro da Conceição.
practical_tips: The visit is peaceful and free — just walk the streets.
Wear sneakers — the hill has climbs.
Avoid very late hours — the neighborhood is quiet but poorly lit at night.
Combine it with a visit to the Valongo Wharf (10-minute walk).
There is a small café on Rua Bela that serves as a meeting point for residents.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Harmonia station (10 min walk).
**On foot:** 15 minutes from the Valongo Wharf.

porto-maravilha.name: Porto Maravilha
porto-maravilha.short_description: Brazil's largest urban PPP operation. It revitalized 5 million m² of the port area between 2009 and 2016.
porto-maravilha.address: Port Area — Saúde, Gamboa, Santo Cristo

content.history:
Porto Maravilha was created by Municipal Law 101 of 23 November 2009, under Mayor Eduardo Paes. It is the largest Consortium Urban Operation in Brazil's history, covering 5 million m² in the Saúde, Gamboa, Santo Cristo and Caju neighborhoods and part of Downtown.

The total investment was R$ 8 billion, structured as a public-private partnership (PPP) — the largest urban PPP in Brazil. The Urban Development Company of the Rio Port Region (CDURP) was created to manage the project.

The main milestones:
- 2011: Works begin; the Valongo Wharf is discovered
- 2013: MAR (Rio Art Museum) opens
- 2013: Demolition of the Perimetral viaduct (3.5 km of elevated highway)
- 2015: The Museum of Tomorrow opens
- 2016: The tram (VLT) is inaugurated (June 2016)
- 2016: The Olympic Boulevard for the Olympic Games
- 2016: AquaRio opens

Besides the cultural facilities, the project included: 700 km of sewage and water networks, fiber-optic installation, 17 km of bike lanes, the burial of 23 km of overhead electrical wiring and the planting of 15,000 trees.

The project was praised internationally for its urban transformation, but also criticized for displacing low-income residents and not solving structural social problems.
curiosities: The demolition of the Perimetral viaduct revealed a waterfront that had been hidden since the 1960s.
Porto Maravilha is the largest urban PPP ever carried out in Brazil.
During the works, besides the Valongo Wharf, other archaeological remains were found — including the bones of enslaved Africans.
The project was criticized for removing residents of favelas such as Morro da Providência to make way for Olympic facilities.
The Olympic Boulevard has one of the largest graffiti murals in the world, by Eduardo Kobra.
practical_tips: The Porto Maravilha area is great for walking — flat, with bike lanes and a sea view.
The Olympic Boulevard runs from Praça Mauá to beyond the Valongo Wharf.
There are modern bars and restaurants in the area — very different from the traditional center.
The tram (VLT) runs throughout the area — the best way to explore.
content.how_to_get_there:

**On foot:** Accessible from Praça Mauá and Central do Brasil.

gamboa-acao.name: Gamboa Ação

content.history: Founded in 2014, Gamboa Ação is a civil-society organization working in Rio's port area, in the Gamboa neighborhood and the Providência community. Its purpose is to develop free, productive, socially integrated and morally responsible individuals, offering holistic education to children and teenagers in situations of social vulnerability. The work is based on principles and values and is organized into three areas — education, art and culture, and sport and leisure — with healthy meals and quality time every day.

muhcab.short_description: A municipal museum in Gamboa, in the Little Africa territory, dedicated to Afro-Brazilian history and culture. Housed in the former José Bonifácio Cultural Center.
muhcab.admission: Free entry (drop-in visits need no booking; guided tours require booking) — confirm on the official website

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm on the official website before going

content.history: The **MUHCAB — Museum of Afro-Brazilian History and Culture** is at Rua Pedro Ernesto, 80, in **Gamboa**, within the historic territory of **Little Africa**, in Rio's port area. The building, built in the late 19th century, once housed the Santa Rita School, the José Bonifácio School and the Gamboa Library; in the 1980s it became the **José Bonifácio Cultural Center**. It opened as MUHCAB in **November 2021**. The museum preserves tangible and intangible heritage and presents Brazil's history through Afro-Brazilian voices and experiences, with exhibitions and themed rooms. It is part of the **African Heritage Circuit Historic-Interpretive Route**, which connects memory sites such as the **Pedra do Sal** and the **Valongo Wharf** (a UNESCO World Heritage Site).
curiosities: The building was the Santa Rita School, the José Bonifácio School and the Gamboa Library before becoming a museum.
In the 1980s it operated as the José Bonifácio Cultural Center.
It's in Little Africa, the historic territory of Black carioca culture.
It is part of the African Heritage Circuit, along with the Pedra do Sal and the Valongo Wharf.
practical_tips: Free entry — drop-in visits need no booking; guided tours must be booked. Confirm on the official website.
The museum is accessible: ramps, elevator, wide corridors and adapted restrooms.
Combine it with the Pedra do Sal and the Valongo Wharf, in the same area.
Approximate coordinate for the address (Rua Pedro Ernesto, 80, Gamboa) — confirm before going, as hours and information may change.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** port-area stations (Gamboa / Saúde).
