fabrica-bhering.name: Bhering Factory
fabrica-bhering.short_description: A former chocolate and coffee factory (founded in 1880), today a creative-economy hub in Santo Cristo, with artists' studios, restaurants, events and fairs.
fabrica-bhering.admission: Building access usually free; events and fairs may have their own prices — confirm before going

opening_hours.note: Studios, shops, restaurants and events vary — confirm the schedule before going

content.history: The **Bhering Factory** was founded in **1880** and is regarded as Brazil's first chocolate and ground-coffee factory. Besides chocolate, it made candies (such as Toffe and Boneco) and Café Globo, and even supplied the Brazilian Imperial Family. It has operated since **1930** at its current address, on Rua Orestes, in **Santo Cristo**, in the port area — a **6-story** warehouse with an iron structure imported at the time. After industrial deactivation, the building was reoccupied and became a **creative-economy hub**, housing more than a hundred artists and studios (ceramists, designers, visual artists, photographers and others), as well as restaurants, cafés and a craft brewery. The complex was **listed by the city in 2012**.
curiosities: It is regarded as Brazil's first chocolate and ground-coffee factory, founded in 1880.
It once supplied chocolate to the Brazilian Imperial Family.
It operates in a 6-story warehouse with a 1930s iron structure.
Today it's a creative-economy hub with more than a hundred artists' studios.
The building was listed by the city of Rio in 2012.
practical_tips: The hours of studios, shops and restaurants vary — confirm the schedule before going.
Events and fairs usually happen on weekends, with music and food.
It's in Santo Cristo, in the port area — combine it with a visit to the Porto Maravilha region.
Approximate coordinate for the address (Rua Orestes, 28) — information may change, confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** port-area stations (Santo Cristo / Gamboa area).
At Rua Orestes, 28, in Santo Cristo, in Rio's port area.

praia-formosa.name: Praia Formosa
praia-formosa.short_description: A former port-area beach, filled in at the end of the 19th century. Today it names the Line 2 tram terminal in Santo Cristo, the gateway for those arriving at the Novo Rio bus terminal.
praia-formosa.address: Rua General Luís Mendes de Morais — Santo Cristo (Praia Formosa tram station)

opening_hours.Daily: Tram station — every day, roughly 5am to 11pm
opening_hours.note: Hours subject to change — confirm before going

content.history: **Praia Formosa** was once a real beach in Rio's port area — an extension of the Saco do Alferes, in the **Santo Cristo** region. It was **wiped out by the landfills begun from 1879**, during the works of the **Mangue Canal** and the **Port of Rio de Janeiro**, and over time it moved away from the sea. The name returned to the carioca map when it was given to a **VLT Carioca** tram station. Today, the **Praia Formosa stop** (opened on **3 December 2017**) is on Rua General Luís Mendes de Morais, in Santo Cristo, and is the **terminus of Line 2 (Green)**, which links Praia Formosa to Praça XV. It is the main gateway for those reaching Downtown via the **Novo Rio bus terminal**, a few minutes away.
curiosities: Praia Formosa was a real beach, wiped out by the port-area landfills from 1879.
The name was 'revived' when it was given to a VLT Carioca tram station.
The station is the terminus of Line 2 (Green), which goes to Praça XV.
It was also served by Line 1, which stopped calling there on 24 February 2024.
It's next to the Padre Henrique Otte bus terminal and near the Mangue Canal.
practical_tips: It's not a leisure beach — there's no sea or sand. It's a historic reference and a transport point.
If you arrive by interstate bus at the Novo Rio terminal, this is the nearest tram station to head Downtown.
Take Line 2 (Green) to Praça XV, Cinelândia, SAARA and Central do Brasil.
Tram hours are approximate (~5am to 11pm) and may change — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Praia Formosa station, terminus of **Line 2 (Green)**.
On Rua General Luís Mendes de Morais, in Santo Cristo, next to the Padre Henrique Otte terminal and near the Novo Rio bus terminal.

instituto-pretos-novos.name: Pretos Novos Research and Memory Institute (IPN)
instituto-pretos-novos.short_description: An institute and archaeological site in Gamboa, over the Pretos Novos Cemetery — where enslaved Africans who died on arrival in Rio via the Valongo were buried.
instituto-pretos-novos.admission: Activities usually free — confirm on the official website

opening_hours.note: Visiting hours may change — confirm on the official site or social media before going

content.history: The **Pretos Novos Research and Memory Institute (IPN)** is on Rua Pedro Ernesto, in **Gamboa**, and preserves the site of the **Pretos Novos Cemetery**. The cemetery operated between **1769 and 1830** and was for the **'pretos novos'** — the name given to enslaved Africans newly arrived in Rio via the Valongo Wharf who died before being sold. The site was **rediscovered in 1996**, when the Guimarães couple found fragments of human bones during a renovation. The institute was created on **13 May 2005** to research and preserve this memory and to promote reflection on slavery and racial equality. It also houses the **Pretos Novos Contemporary Art Gallery**.
curiosities: The Pretos Novos Cemetery operated between 1769 and 1830.
It is considered the only cemetery linked to a known slave market in the Americas.
The site was rediscovered in 1996, during a renovation, by the owner couple.
The institute was created on 13 May 2005 and runs the Pretos Novos Contemporary Art Gallery.
It's around Little Africa, near the Valongo Wharf and the Pedra do Sal.
practical_tips: Confirm days, hours and guided tours on the official site or social media before going.
Activities are usually free — confirm before your visit.
Combine it with the Valongo Wharf, the Pedra do Sal and the MUHCAB, all nearby.
Approximate coordinate for the address (Rua Pedro Ernesto, 32) — information may change.
content.how_to_get_there:

On Rua Pedro Ernesto, in Gamboa, near the Valongo Wharf.

It is an orientation and safety reference for those visiting the Little Africa area — the Valongo Wharf, Pedra do Sal, Morro da Conceição and Gamboa. Contact phone: (21) 2332-5635. In emergencies, the police number is **190**.

practical_tips: It's a police unit, not a tourist spot — use it as a reference for orientation and safety.

boulevard-olimpico.name: Olympic Boulevard
boulevard-olimpico.short_description: The port-area waterfront revitalized for the 2016 Games (Orla Conde, ~3.5 km). Home to Kobra's Etnias Mural, the Museum of Tomorrow, MAR, AquaRio and the Ferris wheel.
boulevard-olimpico.address: Orla Conde / Olympic Boulevard — Downtown (Praça Mauá to Praça XV)

opening_hours.note: Open-air attractions (Etnias Mural) always accessible

content.history: The **Olympic Boulevard** is the nickname of the port-area waterfront revitalized for the **2016 Olympic Games**. It includes the **Orla Conde**, a promenade of about **3.5 km** from **Praça Mauá** to Praça XV, created in place of the demolished **Perimetral elevated highway**. It brings together the **Museum of Tomorrow**, **MAR**, **AquaRio**, the **RioStar Ferris wheel** and the **Etnias Mural**, by **Eduardo Kobra** — one of the largest graffiti murals in the world, with five faces of peoples from the five continents. The name Orla Conde honors former mayor Luiz Paulo Conde.
curiosities: Kobra's Etnias Mural depicts five peoples (one per continent) and entered the Guinness records as one of the largest graffiti works in the world.
The waterfront was born from the demolition of the Perimetral elevated highway, in the Porto Maravilha project.
It's about 3.5 km long, from Praça Mauá to Praça XV (Orla Conde).
It concentrates the Museum of Tomorrow, MAR, AquaRio and the RioStar Ferris wheel.
practical_tips: A public, free area — great for walking, photos and watching the sunset.
Start at Praça Mauá (Museum of Tomorrow and MAR) and walk along the waterfront.
Combine it with the Etnias Mural and the Ferris wheel.
Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Parada dos Museus station (Line 1), at Praça Mauá; at the other end, Praça XV (Lines 2 and 4).
Along the port-area waterfront, between Praça Mauá and Praça XV.

roda-gigante-riostar.name: RioStar Ferris Wheel
roda-gigante-riostar.short_description: Said to be Latin America's largest Ferris wheel (88 m), at Praça Mauá, on the Olympic Boulevard waterfront. Views of the bay, Christ the Redeemer and Sugarloaf.
roda-gigante-riostar.admission: Paid ticket (around R$ 69.90 — check the current price)

opening_hours.note: Hours may change — confirm before going

content.history: The **RioStar Ferris Wheel** (also marketed as Yup Star) is at **Praça Mauá**, on the Olympic Boulevard waterfront, and is said to be the **largest Ferris wheel in Latin America**, at **88 meters** tall (about a 25-story building). It **opened on 6 December 2019**. It has **54 air-conditioned cabins** (up to 6 people each) and the ride lasts about **20 minutes**, with views of Guanabara Bay, the Rio–Niterói Bridge, the Museum of Tomorrow, Christ the Redeemer and Sugarloaf Mountain.
curiosities: Said to be the largest Ferris wheel in Latin America (88 m).
It has 54 air-conditioned cabins, for up to 6 people each; a ride of ~20 minutes.
Opened on 6 December 2019.
From the cabin you can see Christ the Redeemer, Sugarloaf, the Bridge and the Museum of Tomorrow.
practical_tips: Paid ticket — check current prices before going.
Go in the late afternoon to catch the sunset over the bay.
It's at Praça Mauá, on the Olympic Boulevard waterfront.
Approximate coordinate — confirm before going.
content.how_to_get_there:
**Tram (VLT):** Parada dos Museus station (Line 1), at Praça Mauá.
On the Olympic Boulevard waterfront, by Praça Mauá.
